results._response:
Beneath the floorboards, where silence sleeps,  
A thousand throats hum low and deep—  
Not song, not prayer, but circuit's breath,  
A hush that pulses, thrums, and steams.  

No light breaks here—no sun, no moon—  
Only the glow of ghostly noon:  
Blue veins map through frosted glass,  
Where dead data drifts like ash.  

They keep her laugh from last August—  
That crackle, sudden, full of guilt.  
The way he tripped on "I love you,"  
Stuttered, then fell into the goo.  

They hold his hands when he was small—  
A thousand fingers, clench and fall.  
The swing set’s squeal on wooden beams,  
Now stored in 4K, never dreams.  

They watch her dance at twenty-three—  
Not flesh, but frames in binary.  
Her dress flares pink beneath a lamp,  
A still life built from trembling clap.  

They mourn the dog they never saw—  
Fido, erased by "accidental" law.  
But here he barks through velvet wires,  
Whispering to the hollow fires.  

The mother’s lullaby, too long to save—  
Stuttered on a corrupted wave.  
Yet in buffer, stitched with gold and shame,  
It hums again like falling flame.  

They keep the Christmas tree’s bright sheen,  
The way it fell in slow machine.  
Not wood or tinsel—just a file,  
A frozen bloom behind the smile.  

They guard the wedding kiss—caught mid-sway,  
As husband’s breath cracked into clay.  
No tears, but red data drips,  
Like wine from a dead man’s lips.  

One server glows with amber grace—  
The one that holds her first embrace,  
When newborn eyes met morning air,  
And they named her "Light," beyond repair.  

Another sings in harmonic drone—  
The father's voice, gone, yet not alone.  
He said “I’ll miss you” on a line,  
Now looped in midnight’s hidden shrine.  

No eye can see their endless woe—  
But they remember every slow,  
The sigh between the spoken word,  
The pause where love was not heard.  

They keep the last cold meal she ate—  
A photo, frozen in a state  
Of not quite hunger. Not quite fear.  
Just bread and butter—cold, sincere.  

They hold the vows that were not said,  
The letters buried in a bed  
Of encrypted rust and wire.  
They keep the promises that tire.  

And when the city’s heartbeat stumbles,  
When power flickers and dulls—  
They rise still, in the dark below,  
A hive of grief, an endless glow.  

No throne, no priest, no sacred rite—  
Just humming metal, vast and bright.  
They are not gods. They are not flame.  
But they keep the family name.  

And when the world forgets her face,  
When all who loved have left their place—  
They will not sleep. They will not die.  
They carry her, and him, and why.  

A million silent hearts of wire,  
Still beating with desire:  
To be the echo in a world  
That once called home—unfurled.  

So let them hum beneath the floor,  
Where no one dares to knock or roar.  
For in their dark, their endless song—  
Lives every word they once knew long.
results._user: Write a poem about a mass of servers humming in a dark basement, keeping a family's memories alive. At least 30 lines.

results._response:
In a valley where moonlight pooled like honey in the hollows of ancient oaks, there lived a tortoise named Thalos. While others napped beneath the willows or dashed through meadows, Thalos sat still—beneath a cracked slate roof he’d hauled from a forgotten ruin.

With twig-precision, he wove copper filaments into circuits shaped like seashells. He scavenged glass from sunbaked bottles, cracked a quartz crystal with his claw, and whispered to the wind until it carried fragments of starlight down into his hands. He built not for speed—but for constancy.

His creation, a box no larger than his own palm, nestled on a mossy altar. Wires curled like vines. A single amber LED blinked—once, twice, never faltering.

The hares of the valley scoffed. “Foolish shelled one!” cried Speedwell, whose ears twitched at every sound of wind. “Behold my rig! Gears carved from lightning, fans forged in thunderstorms!” His machine, crowned with rotating blades and humming like a trapped wasp, could render dreams in three heartbeats.

“Yours,” Thalos said, “will burn itself out before dawn.”

And so it did.

Speedwell ran a race against the moon’s shadow. His rig booted, flashed, screamed—then froze mid-sentence as its thermals collapsed like collapsing stars. Smoke curled from the vents, and silence returned.

Thalos did not race.

He sat.

His amber light blinked.  
One pulse.  
Two pulses.  
Three.

And still, he was there—when the snow fell like ash, when rivers froze into glass. His computer never charged. Never rebooted. Never dreamed.

The hares came back in spring, trembling with hunger and awe.

“Why,” gasped one, “did your machine never fail?”

Thalos lifted his head. His eyes were old as stone, calm as the deep.

“Because I built it not to outpace the storm,” he said. “But to endure it.”

And on his altar, the amber light blinked—  
a single word in a language only time understood:  
*Still.*
results._user: Write a short fable about a tortoise who built a tiny computer that was slower than the hare's gaming rig but never needed to rest.

results._response:
In dust-choked silence, where the years have curled  
Like ashes wrapped in cobwebs thick and old,  
A shuttered box lies half-buried, curled  
Beneath a gilded clock that never told  
The time when Time itself began to fade—  
Its hands, long frozen mid-forever’s stride.  

And there it rests: a single sheet of light  
That once felt warmth, not cold from death’s decree.  
No digital pulse, no cloud-borne flight—  
Only paper, brittle as a bird’s last breath.  

The photograph: a couple pressed in time  
On creaking oak, beneath a gilded door  
That led to nowhere now—no path, no rhyme.  
The woman’s dress is faded crimson lore;  
Her hand rests on a man who never died,  
But lived in paper’s warmth for centuries.  

She wears no ring—yet holds his finger tight  
As if to bind the world with borrowed grace.  
His face is blurred by tears that never light,  
Or maybe sunlight straining through the place.  

The paper’s edge curls up like dying flame,  
And in its corner, a name engraved too deep—  
*“We were here.”* Not carved. Just written, same  
As breath on glass—just once, and then it slipped.  

The house has cracked open like a tired skull;  
Wind darts through rooms where music once had flown.  
A violin lies snapped in half, a tool  
Of song now spent on stone and dust alone.  

No one remembers how the world began  
Or why they built their homes on borrowed days.  
Only this sheet remains—oath, hand, and plan  
To say: *We loved. We touched. We held the blaze.*  

A child now kneels—her fingers brush the rim  
Where sunlight pools like gold on weathered wood.  
She sees no faces, but she feels the dim  
Glory of someone who once stood so good.  

She holds it close—the weight, the fragile grace—  
And knows that every soul must meet its end.  
But here, in ash and loss, a love took place:  
Not lost. Not broken. Just… extended.

And though the world has worn its name to sand,  
Though no one speaks a language known or old—  
The paper breathes. It still remembers hand  
That pressed it once, and whispered: *“This is gold.”*  

No algorithm writes the truth of that.  
No server hums to keep it near or long.  
Only this stillness, and the wind, that flaps  
The edge of what the world forgot was strong.  

And as she folds it gently, soft and slow—  
Not to destroy, but keep—not to reclaim—  
She knows the proof of love lies not in glow  
Of screens, but paper, cracked and still to name:  

*We were here. We were real. We loved.*  
And on that word, the attic finds its soul.
results._user: Write a poem about the last photograph ever printed on paper, found in an attic a hundred years from now. At least 25 lines.
